data.title: A Look At Banco Di Desio E Della Brianza (BIT:BDB) Valuation After Q4 Earnings And 2026 Dividend Update
data.description:
Banco di Desio e della Brianza (BIT:BDB) has drawn fresh attention after reporting fourth quarter net income of €21.41 million and declaring an annual dividend of €0.5105 per share for 2026.

See our latest analysis for Banco di Desio e della Brianza.

The recent dividend announcement and fourth quarter earnings update come after a mixed price pattern, with a 1-day share price return of 2.24% at €8.69 contrasting with a 7.36% year to date decline. At the same time, a 1-year total shareholder return of 19.43% and a roughly 3x total shareholder return over five years suggest long term momentum has been strong even as short term sentiment has cooled.

If this bank’s move has you thinking about what else might be interesting in the market, it could be a good time to broaden your search with 104 top founder-led companies.

With Banco di Desio e della Brianza offering a €0.5105 dividend per share and reporting fourth quarter net income of €21.41 million, you might be wondering whether the current share price is leaving upside on the table or already reflecting future growth.

Preferred P/E of 9x: Is it justified?

On a simple earnings basis, Banco di Desio e della Brianza trades on a P/E of 9x at a last close of €8.69, which is lower than both the Italian market and banking peers.

P/E compares the current share price to the bank’s earnings per share, so a lower P/E can suggest the market is assigning a lower price tag to each euro of profit. For a bank that has reported net income of €127.309 million and has grown earnings by 22.4% per year over the past 5 years, that sets an interesting reference point for readers assessing how much future earnings strength is already in the price.

Relative to the Italian market P/E of 17.5x, BDB’s 9x multiple is considerably lower, and it is also below both the peer average of 10.3x and the wider European banks average of 10.8x. That combination means the shares are priced at a discount to earnings across several comparison sets, even though earnings growth over the past year of 3.9% has been slower than the bank’s own 5 year average and below the 8.7% reported for the banks industry.
